comments: TV mentions Steve Hackett is finishing his set tonight at Reading and Greg Lake plays on Sunday
Starfighters are doing next week's session
TV's links after the Van Halen track are missing and the show wrap-up is missing too. The run time suggests the play list is complete.

sessions.details: recorded for Friday Rock Show. First TX.

track_listing.details: LP-The Power of Rock and Roll

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the Radio here, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to The Friday Rock Show.
Right, this rather excellent summer's night, we have a special session for you.
One that's built around tracks from Bernie Marsden's latest solo LP.
It features Bernie himself, of course, and Don Airy, Neil Murray, Simon Phillips, and David Coverdale.
Got the classics by Supertramp, Steve Hackett, Van Halen, Rush and Trust.
And some new releases from Nazareth, Billy Squire, Rolling Stones, 38 Special, Rose Tattoo, Greg Lake, The Tangs, The Starfighters, Bobby and Frank Marino.
And a Friday Night Connection. That's the answer to last week's.
But tonight, we start with this.
That's the new single by Nazareth. It's Morning Dew.
It is, of course, a record that was a big hit for Tim Rose.
though the person who originally wrote it is a Canadian lady
who now lives in Devon by the name of Bonnie Dobson.
And it's out on NEMS records.
Before that, he heard something from the classic album called
The Who, live at Leeds.
Summertime Blues, the old Eddie Cotteran number.
It's 11 minutes after 10.
I'd like to say hello to Christopher.
Christopher lives at No. 1A Princess Street, Hindley, near Wigan in Lancs,
and his family think the world of him.
A lady by the name of Debbie has sent me this little communication
to tell me that Heinrich, her boyfriend, is 21 today.
She loves you.
And Jan, who lives at number three Mall and Road, I think it is,
Hilltop, Ebervale and Gwent,
at one time was on holiday about a couple of weeks ago, I think,
with his girlfriend Louise.
They were camping, but now I think they're back home.
Hi to both of you.
And this is Trust.
Please cry the truth.
The brush, it is to stay honest.
It was a saint Messiah.
Cry for you, okay, see my tears.
Turn to my shadow.
...still burning.
...in my confidence in liberty.
...the latest single to be put out in this country by Billy Squire.
Billy Squire, on Capitol Records, and my kind of lover.
And before that, something from the album Refreshing by the French band called Trust.
English lyrics put together by Jimmy Percy.
It was out some time ago on CBS.
The fact you heard was Paris is still burning.
Both of those bands are appearing at Reading tomorrow.
I'm really looking forward to seeing both of them.
And the first time I saw Billy Squire was when he was over here before doing a support.
And he was good.
A little nervous.
I think now he's got some big success in America.
He'll have a lot more.
Get up and go.
I've never seen Trust, and I'm really looking forward to seeing them tomorrow night at Reading.
They're a heavy band.
Postcard here from someplace in France that I won't even bother to try to pronounce.
It was sent on the 19th of the 8th.
I think it's Niva.
Anyway, it's from Mark Bell.
That's easier to say.
He wants me to say hello to Stephen and Stuart and Niven and his mother
and Zoe and most especially a lady by the name of Denise.
Liz lives at Rose Cottage, Levenbridge, Yarm in Cleveland.
Philip Martin is her boyfriend.
And I just hope that the O-level results were positive.
A little card here from Jill of Longview, Coopers Hill in Gloucester.
Dear Tommy Vance, please play a record for my boyfriend, Phil Lloyd,
who isn't going to university next year after all.
It might cheer him up a bit. I didn't want him to go anyway.
It's 10.21. Here's the first of four from our, well, I've got to say, super session.
It was put together by Don Airey, Neil Murray, Simon Phillips and David Coverdale
and Mr Bernie Marsden in the BBC studios.
I purposefully haven't listened to this session yet
because I just want to sit here and wallow in it.
Absolutely impeccable band they are.
That's a track from their last studio album called Breakfast in America.
It came out in 1979.
The band are called Supertramp and that track has the title Child of Vision.
And I would reckon it's about time for another studio album from that band
and also a visit to this country.
To sit in the audience and watch them perform is pure joy.
That track was chosen by Mike,
who lives at No. 5 Oathills, Caution, in Wilts.
He wants me to say hello to everybody,
particularly Aldo and Warren and Dougie and Pete
and all the ex-fourth-year electrical engineers
of I think it's the Hyerick Watt University in Edinburgh
and also all the Suzuki Owners Club in Edinburgh as well.
Great choice of music, Michael.
Andrew Floyd Martin,
our 58 Orchard Road, Errington in Birmingham.
He likes the program, loves the music,
hates the disc jockey, don't blame him.
But I mention, please, Chris the Cat,
Joe the Mighty Organ, really,
Paul the...
What?
John alias Stumpy,
and Bernie alias Mr. Respectable,
but I don't believe that,
and Mary the Matchbox,
and Liz the Sheepdog.
And before the Supertramp track,
you heard the first of four
from our special Bernie Marsden session.
That was the studio version of a thing that he's got out as a single,
which I really enjoy because it's extremely commercial at one end,
at the beginning, and then it goes into this great rock guitar,
just like Bernie knows how to lay down.
That's 38 Special. It's their new single.
It's out on A&M Records. It's called The First Time Around.
They're appearing at Reading on Sunday.
If you get the chance to see them, do their great.
Before that, you heard The Rolling Stones,
a track from their album called Tattoo You.
It's out on their own label.
the fact you heard was called Slave.
I got a really excellent letter from a lady by the name of Mary
who lives in Wistow in Huntingdon.
I'm not going to tell you what the gist of the letter is,
but I must say that I appreciate what was written
as being an experience that she obviously felt quite sincere about.
Just a quote from her letter, she says,
So, to get to the point, if anybody knows a 19-year-old guy from London called John,
who's 6'4", bearded, and has a sister called Susie,
and friends Paul, Keith and Tracy, who were all with him at Donington,
will they please tell him, that's John,
that I can't meet him at Hammersmith on the 14th to see Schenker
because my tickets are for the 13th, and that I'm disappointed.
Great human letter, that, Mary.
And John, if you're listening, if I were you, I'd be standing outside there on the 13th,
because that lady likes you.
I'm Bernie Marsden.
You just heard a track from the brand new album by Rose Tattoo,
who are doing Reading this Saturday.
They'll be at the Reading Festival this Saturday, and I'll be watching them.
Their new album is called Assault and Battery.
It's out on Carrere Records.
The track you heard was Sidewalk Sally.
Prior to that, something from our excellent session by Bernie Marsden.
The track that you'll find on his current solo album,
the track was called Who's Foolin' Who.
Back to the Friday Night Connection.
Okay, the tracks we played you last week were these.
The Faith Healer by Alex Harvey,
or the sensational Alex Harvey Band,
to give them their full title of that particular era.
It was out on Vertigo, and it came from the album called Next.
Then we played a track from Slow Hand by Eric Clapton,
a track that's called Next Time You See Her.
And then we played Baba O'Reilly by The Who from the album Who's Next,
an album by Alex Harvey called Next, Next Time You See Her,
and Who's Next, keyword next.
Winner, Salem Fredericks, a 57, Derby Grove, Lenton, Nottingham.
Gets himself a 10-pound record token.
Good on you, Salem.
Got to own up.
Virtually everybody got it right.
congratulations to, well, virtually
everybody, but unfortunately, as always,
there's only one prize, because we're skipped.
Brassic Lint, you know what I mean,
got no poppy, got no money, well,
you know, what the economy's like,
but the music's good, and this is the music
that was put together by Salem. Three bits of
music, there's a connection contained
therein.
Touch down
and let
strangers
know
silence
We'll be right back.
From the album Cured, it was called Picture Postcard.
I guess he's just about come off stage at Reading.
Missed him tonight, never mind.
Always played the records, I guess.
And they're always good to listen to.
And before that, something from an album that's going to be coming out shortly
by a guy who's going to present his new band to the United Kingdom
this Sunday night at Reading.
It's the new Greg Lake band, Nuclear Tack was the track.
I think they're really heavy, I've not heard that before.
Looking forward to seeing them Sunday night.
Little letter here from Tony, the one with the long hair,
and Paul, the one with the muscles, Clark.
Live at eight Chaucer Close, Popley One, Blazing Stoke, and Hanson.
I'm going to do something for them
and for everybody who's going to go to the Schenker gig
at Southampton on September 9.
Couple of people by the name of Young,
that's Paul, a young and a role man,
who live at number 33, Morven Place, Dalgety Bay,
I think it is, Fife in Scotland,
are, well, celebrating their 15th wedding anniversary.
Or at least they started celebrating a yesterpin.
I'm sure they're continuing today.
Congratulations to both of you.
And Paula says to her old man,
still love him very much and thanks for everything.
The nice thing about doing this type of show
is there's an incredible spread of people in terms of age.
Music love us all.
Bernie Marsden.
That's the new single by Tangerine Dream.
It's Alan Virgin.
It's called Coronzon.
And before that, something from our session by Bernie Marsden.
and also featuring Don Airy and Neil Murray and Simon Phillips and David Coverdale.
Tasty Session.
Good track.
It's quite an old song, actually.
I think it's an old Tamla Motown song called Shaky Ground.
Good version.
Dedicate that to Jill and to Alison from Burton-on-Trent,
from a gentleman who met both those ladies at Castle Donington last week,
a fellow by the name of Derek,
who says, tell them thanks for making a great day that much better.
I'd also like to say hello to John Ozzie Malone,
Pete Filthy Animal Creedon,
Rob, Don't Knock the Bald Head, Barrett, and Mark Faldiano, Clone, Celia, and all the heavy Harlow headbangers.
Also, Dave College as well.
What's for me to say to Gary Owens?
She loves you.
Simple as that.
Little card here from The Hobbit.
Prospect Lane, Wesley and Hammond.
Could you play?
Or dedicate a track, he says here, to my wife, Anne, who's being released from Queen's Hotel on the 28th of August today.
Tell her I love her a lot.
She never believes me.
A little trust here and there.
And here's one from Caerphilly, in Midglemorgan, South Wales, when I say, hello to all the headbangers in Caerphilly, from Diamond and Hawk and Josh, and his girlfriend Sue.
Starfighters are going to be doing a session on our show next week. Here's a track from their album called Starfighters.
Ain't Talking About Love came out in 1978, and that was specially for a gentleman whom I'm told is spending an enforced holiday in Swansea.
A fellow by the name of Headbanging Tim
Best wishes from all his mates
Including Hilary and Sarah and Jenny and Roz
And Rob and numerous others
And before that something from a band
Who are doing a session for us next week
From their album Starfighters
It was Starfighters and Devil's Driving
